questions.question: What is the Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)?
questions.answer:
SRP states that a class should have only one reason to change — it should do one thing and do it well. "One reason to change" is more precise than "one thing": a class has one responsibility, so only one type of requirement change should require modifying it.

Violation example:

// BAD: Three reasons to change (data access, business logic, email)
public class InvoiceService
{
    public void ProcessInvoice(Invoice invoice)
    {
        // 1. DB access
        _db.SaveInvoice(invoice);

        // 2. Business logic
        if (invoice.Amount > 10000) ApplyDiscount(invoice);

        // 3. Email sending
        _smtp.SendEmail(invoice.ClientEmail, "Invoice generated");
    }
}

Fixed:

// Three classes, each with one responsibility
public class InvoiceRepository    { void Save(Invoice i) { ... } }
public class InvoicePricingService { void ApplyDiscount(Invoice i) { ... } }
public class InvoiceNotifier     { Task SendReceiptAsync(Invoice i) { ... } }

public class InvoiceProcessor  // orchestrates, doesn't do the work itself
{
    public async Task ProcessAsync(Invoice invoice)
    {
        _repo.Save(invoice);
        _pricing.ApplyDiscount(invoice);
        await _notifier.SendReceiptAsync(invoice);
    }
}

In the PaymentReminder project, I violated SRP initially by having a single Worker class that fetched invoices, built emails, and called SMTP. Splitting into InvoiceFetcher, EmailBuilder, and EmailSender made each unit independently testable and easier to maintain.

followUps.question: How does SRP relate to cohesion?
followUps.answer: SRP is essentially a prescription for high cohesion — all the pieces of a class should belong together and serve the same purpose. A class with high cohesion has all its methods and fields related to its one responsibility. When you see a class with methods that have little to do with each other (e.g., SaveToDb() and SendEmail() in the same class), it has low cohesion and likely violates SRP. High cohesion makes classes easier to understand, test, and change in isolation. SRP is the design principle; cohesion is the measurable property that results from following it.

followUps.question: How do you know when a class violates SRP?
followUps.answer: Several practical signals: (1) The "and" test — if you describe the class with "and" ("it saves data AND sends emails AND validates input"), each "and" is a separate responsibility. (2) Too many imports/dependencies — a class injecting a repository, an email service, a payment gateway, and a logger in the constructor is doing too much. (3) Frequent changes for different reasons — if the class changes every time marketing wants a different email AND every time the DB schema changes, it has multiple responsibilities. (4) Hard to name — if you can't name the class without using "Manager", "Helper", or "Utility", it's likely doing too much.

questions.tip: Red flag for SRP violation: If describing the class's purpose requires the word "and" — "it saves invoices AND sends emails AND applies discounts" — it violates SRP. Each "and" is a separate responsibility.

questions.question: What is the Open/Closed Principle (OCP)?
questions.answer:
OCP states that classes should be open for extension but closed for modification. You should be able to add new behavior without changing existing, tested code.

Violation: adding a new notification type requires modifying existing code:

// BAD: every new notification type requires modifying this method
public void SendNotification(string type, string message)
{
    if (type == "email")  SendEmail(message);
    else if (type == "sms")  SendSms(message);
    else if (type == "push")  SendPush(message); // had to add this
}

Fixed with polymorphism:

// GOOD: add new types by adding new classes, not modifying existing ones
public interface INotificationSender
{
    string Type { get; }
    Task SendAsync(string message, CancellationToken ct);
}

public class EmailSender : INotificationSender { ... }
public class SmsSender : INotificationSender { ... }
public class PushSender : INotificationSender { ... }  // NEW: no existing code changes

public class NotificationRouter
{
    private readonly IEnumerable<INotificationSender> _senders;

    public async Task SendAsync(string type, string message, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var sender = _senders.Single(s => s.Type == type);
        await sender.SendAsync(message, ct);
    }
}

In the PaymentReminder service I used this pattern for notification channels. Adding WhatsApp notifications later required only a new WhatsAppSender class — zero changes to existing code.

followUps.question: How do you implement OCP using the Strategy pattern?
followUps.answer: The Strategy pattern is one of the cleanest OCP implementations. Define a strategy interface (IDiscountStrategy), create concrete strategies for each behavior (SeasonalDiscountStrategy, LoyaltyDiscountStrategy), and inject IEnumerable<IDiscountStrategy> into the context class. Adding a new discount type means adding a new class and registering it — zero changes to existing strategies or the context. In .NET DI, register all strategies and select at runtime by a discriminator property. The context never needs a switch statement.

followUps.question: Is OCP realistic — don't you always have to modify something?
followUps.answer: Yes, when requirements change, you almost always modify something. OCP is not about never modifying code — it's about identifying the right extension points in advance and designing them so adding new behavior extends rather than modifies. You can't anticipate every change, so you apply OCP selectively to the parts most likely to change (payment methods, notification channels, report formats). For everything else, simpler code without premature abstractions is better. Uncle Bob calls this "closed with respect to the kinds of changes that are anticipated."

questions.tip: OCP is about anticipated change points. You can't make everything extensible without modification. Identify where requirements are likely to change (notification types, payment methods, export formats) and design those extension points. The rest can be closed.

questions.question: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)?
questions.answer:
LSP states that if S is a subtype of T, then anywhere you use T, you can substitute S without breaking the program. Subclasses must honor the contract of the base class.

Classic violation — the Square/Rectangle problem:

public class Rectangle
{
    public virtual int Width { get; set; }
    public virtual int Height { get; set; }
    public int Area() => Width * Height;
}

public class Square : Rectangle  // LSP violation!
{
    public override int Width  { set { base.Width = base.Height = value; } }
    public override int Height { set { base.Width = base.Height = value; } }
}

// Consumer that expects Rectangle contract is broken:
void ResizeAndCalculate(Rectangle r)
{
    r.Width = 5;
    r.Height = 10;
    Assert(r.Area() == 50);  // FAILS for Square: area is 100!
}

Fix: Don't use inheritance here — Square and Rectangle are not substitutable. Use separate classes or an IShape interface.

Practical LSP checks:

  • Derived class should not throw exceptions the base doesn't
  • Derived class should not strengthen preconditions or weaken postconditions
  • Derived class should not remove behaviors the base provides

followUps.question: What is the precondition/postcondition rule in LSP?
followUps.answer: Derived classes must not strengthen preconditions (require stricter input than the base). If a base method accepts any integer, a derived override cannot demand a positive integer — callers who use the base class contract would be surprised. Derived classes must not weaken postconditions (guarantee less than the base). If the base guarantees a non-null return, the derived must also guarantee non-null. These rules ensure that code written against the base class continues to work correctly when given a derived class instance — the foundation of substitutability.

followUps.question: How does LSP relate to interface segregation?
followUps.answer: LSP and ISP both address substitutability but at different levels. LSP is about class inheritance — derived classes must be substitutable for base classes. ISP is about interface contracts — clients should not depend on methods they don't use (which would force them to pass objects that implement unused methods). When an interface is too broad, a class implementing it may need to throw NotImplementedException for unused methods — an LSP violation at the interface level. Applying ISP (smaller, focused interfaces) naturally reduces LSP violations because each implementer only commits to the methods it can genuinely support.

questions.tip: LSP in practice: If you find yourself checking if (obj is DerivedClass) before calling methods, you're violating LSP — the derived class isn't truly substitutable. Refactor to polymorphism instead of type checks.

questions.question: What is the Interface Segregation Principle (ISP)?
questions.answer:
ISP states that clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they don't use. Fat interfaces should be split into smaller, more focused ones.

Violation — fat interface:

// BAD: every implementer must implement methods they don't need
public interface IWorker
{
    void Work();
    void Eat();  // robots don't eat!
    void Sleep(); // robots don't sleep!
}

public class RobotWorker : IWorker
{
    public void Work() { /* actual logic */ }
    public void Eat() => throw new NotImplementedException();  // forced!
    public void Sleep() => throw new NotImplementedException(); // forced!
}

Fixed — segregated interfaces:

public interface IWorkable  { void Work(); }
public interface IFeedable  { void Eat(); }
public interface ISleepable { void Sleep(); }

public class HumanWorker : IWorkable, IFeedable, ISleepable
{
    public void Work()  { ... }
    public void Eat()   { ... }
    public void Sleep() { ... }
}

public class RobotWorker : IWorkable  // only what it needs
{
    public void Work() { ... }
}

In my services layer: instead of one IUserService with 20 methods, I have IUserAuthService, IUserProfileService, and IUserNotificationService. A controller that only needs auth doesn't depend on profile or notification methods.

followUps.question: How does ISP relate to cohesion?
followUps.answer: ISP drives interface cohesion — a well-segregated interface groups only closely related methods that clients naturally need together. When an interface has low cohesion (unrelated methods forced together), some implementers must throw NotImplementedException for methods they don't support, and clients must depend on methods they'll never call. High cohesion in interfaces means each interface represents a single capability or role. ISP and SRP apply the same cohesion thinking at different levels: SRP to classes, ISP to interfaces.

followUps.question: How fine-grained should interfaces be?
followUps.answer: The right size is one that matches a client's actual usage pattern. If client A only needs read operations and client B only needs write operations, split into IReadRepository and IWriteRepository. But don't create an interface per method — that's excessive granularity with no benefit. A practical guideline: interfaces should be defined from the client's perspective, not the implementer's. Ask "what does this client actually need?" and create the interface that matches that need. Role interfaces (what a client sees) are generally more focused than implementation interfaces (what a class can do).

questions.tip: NotImplementedException is a red flag for ISP violation. If an implementer throws NotImplementedException for interface methods, the interface is too broad and should be split.

questions.question: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)?
questions.answer:
DIP has two rules:

  1. High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules. Both should depend on abstractions.
  2. Abstractions should not depend on details. Details (implementations) should depend on abstractions.

Without DIP:

// BAD: high-level InvoiceService directly depends on low-level EmailSender
public class InvoiceService
{
    private readonly SmtpEmailSender _emailSender = new SmtpEmailSender();  // concrete!

    public async Task CreateInvoiceAsync(Invoice invoice)
    {
        await _db.SaveAsync(invoice);
        await _emailSender.SendAsync(invoice.ClientEmail, "Invoice created");
        // Can't swap to SendGrid without changing InvoiceService
    }
}

With DIP:

// GOOD: both depend on the abstraction IEmailSender
public class InvoiceService
{
    private readonly IEmailSender _emailSender;  // abstraction

    public InvoiceService(IEmailSender emailSender)  // injected
        => _emailSender = emailSender;

    public async Task CreateInvoiceAsync(Invoice invoice)
    {
        await _db.SaveAsync(invoice);
        await _emailSender.SendAsync(invoice.ClientEmail, "Invoice created");
        // Swap SmtpEmailSender → SendGridEmailSender: zero changes here
    }
}

// In tests: inject FakeEmailSender — no SMTP needed

DIP is what makes Dependency Injection work. The DI container wires up the concrete implementations; your business code never sees them.

followUps.question: How does DIP relate to DI (Dependency Injection)?
followUps.answer: DIP is the principle — depend on abstractions, not concretions. DI is one mechanism to achieve it. DIP says "high-level modules should not depend on low-level ones" — but it doesn't specify how the abstraction gets wired up. Constructor injection (the DI pattern) is the cleanest way to satisfy DIP: the class declares its abstract dependencies in the constructor, and an external entity (the DI container or a test) provides the concrete implementation. DIP without DI is possible (factories, service locators) but harder to test and maintain.

followUps.question: What is inversion of control (IoC)?
followUps.answer: IoC is the broader principle that control of object creation and lifecycle is inverted — instead of classes creating their own dependencies, an external entity (the IoC container, a framework, or a test harness) controls creation and wiring. The Hollywood Principle captures it: "Don't call us, we'll call you." DIP is the specific design rule that motivates IoC — by depending on abstractions, classes give up control of which concrete type they use, and that control is handed to whoever assembles the object graph. ASP.NET Core's DI container is the IoC container that implements this inversion for the entire application.

questions.tip: DIP ≠ DI. DIP is the principle (depend on abstractions). DI is one mechanism to achieve it. You could satisfy DIP with the Service Locator pattern too (though it's an anti-pattern). DI via constructor injection is the cleanest implementation of DIP.
